text: FOLQUIN (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Audruick. Pop. 1,027.
FOM-AS-SOHL, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash, and 142 m. SE of Bagdad, and 85 m. NW of Korna, on the l. bank of the Tigris.
FOMEN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Ghilan, district of Rudbar, 15 m. WSW of Reshd, on an affluent of the gulf of Enzelle. Pop. about 5,000. It has a well-frequented market.
FOMETOC. See Bembatooka.
FOMPEDRAZA, or FONPEDRAZA, a small town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 36 m. ESE of Valladolid, partido and 5 m. W of Penafiel, on the r. bank of an affluent of the Duraton. Pop. 192.
FONACZA, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Bihar, near its SE confines, 15 m. SE of Belenges.
FONAMI, a town of Japan, in the island of Kiusiu, prov. of Tzikouzen, 36 m. NNE of Sanga.
FONCINE-LE-HAUT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Jura, cant. of Les Planches. Pop. 1,609. Clocks and implements of various kinds are manufactured here.
FOND-DE-FORET, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, arrond. of Foret. Pop. 313.
FOND-DE-GOTTES, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, arrond. of Ayeneux. Pop. 145.

FOND-DU-LAC, a county of the state of Wisconsin, U. S., comprising an area of 400 sq. m., bordered on the N by the Fox river, and on the NE by Lake Winnebago; and drained by Rock and Milwaukie rivers, and Crocodile and Martin's creeks. It is chiefly prairie. Pop. in 1840, 139.—Its capital, of the same name, is 98 m. NE of Madison, at the head of Winnebago lake.
FOND-DU-LAC (BAY), an arm of Lake Superior, comprising the SW extremity of the lake. It receives the river St. Louis and several minor streams.
FOND-DU-LAC (FORT), a settlement in British North America, in the district of Athabasca, and on the NE side of the lake of that name.
FOND-DE-L'EAU, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, arrond. of Couvin. Pop. 89.— Also a commune in the arrond. of Vesches. Pop. 64.
FOND-DE-LUSTIN, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, arrond. of Lustin. Pop. 346.
FOND-DE-MALONNE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, arrond. of Malonne. Pop. 209.

FOND-DE-NEGRES, a town of Hayti, in the dep. du Sud, 60 m. WSW of Port-au-Prince, and 45 m. ENE of Cayes, near the l. bank of a river of the same name, which throws itself into the Caribbean sea under the name of Côtes-de-Fer.
FOND-DU-VILLAGE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, arrond. of Flémalle-Grande. Pop. 650.

FONDA, a village in Montgomery co., Mohawk township, in the state of New York, U.S., 42 m. W of 2 B Albany, on the N side of Mohawk river, and intersected by the Utica and Schenectady railway. Pop. in 1840, 350.
FONDERIE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Saint-Etienne-de-Baigorry. Pop. 1,419.
FONDETTES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et-Loire, cant. and 4 m. NNW of Tours. Pop. 2,471. It has manufactories of bricks and of vermicelli, and produces good wine.

FONDI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavoro, district and 15 m. NW of Gaeta, and 60 m. NW of Naples, near the frontier of the Pontifical states, and 6 m. from the Mediterranean. Pop. 5,000. It is intersected by the Appian way, which is still well preserved, and forms the principal street; and contains a cathedral, a college, several convents, and charitable institutions, a custom-house, and the remains of Cyclopæan walls. The environs are fertile, but marshy and insalubrious. Olives and citrons are extensively cultivated in the locality, which is also noted for its wine. In the vicinity are the fine fountain of Petronio and ruins of ancient baths. Between the town and the Mediterranean is a lake of the same name, the Lacus Fundanus of the ancients, which discharges itself by two outlets into the sea. Its banks are covered with myrtle and poplars.—F., the Fundi of the Romans, was destroyed by fire in 1222 by Frederick II., and has frequently suffered from incursions of the Turks.
FONDO, a maritime town of Japan, in the prov. of Figo, on the N coast of the island of Amakusa, to the W of the island of Kiusiu, and 39 m. SE of Nagasaki.
FONDO, or Scoglio-Fondo, an island of the Adriatic, in Tyrol, in the circle of Trieste, NE of Male.
FONDOUK, a village and military station in Algeria, in the prov. and 28 m. SE of Algiers, on the road to Hamisi.
FONDS (LES), a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant, arrond. of Braine-le-Chateau. Pop. 94.—Also a commune in the prov. of Namur, arrond. of Gesves. Pop. 272.
FONDS-DE-LESVE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, arrond. of Lesve. Pop. 684.
FONDS-VANDEN-BOSCH-ET-JOLI-BOIS, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant, arrond. of Waterloo. Pop. 247.
FONDSGRAVE, a village of France, in the dep. of the Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Monclar, 9 m. W of Villeneuve-d'Agen, on the r. bank of the Lot. Pop. 1,359.
FONFRIA, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 20 m. WNW of Zamora, partido of Alcanices, near the Portuguese frontier. Pop. 260. It has a custom-house.
FONGA, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Yamato, 96 m. ESE of Osaka.
FONNI, a parish and town of the island of Sardinia, prov. and 14 m. S of Nuoro. Pop. 2,764. It has manufactories of carpets and coverlets.
FONOMI, or Song-Po, one of the 15 districts which compose the prov. of Tsikouzen, in Japan.
FONS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Lot, cant. and 7 m. NW of Figeac. Pop. 1,056.

FONSAGRADA, or FUENSAGRADA (SANTA-MARIA-DE), a judicial partido and town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. of Lugo. The partido comprises 67 parishes. Pop. of town, 451. FONSECA (GULF OF). or CONCHAGUA, a large gulf on the W coast of Central America, opening between Amapala-point (c) on the NW, and Conseguina-point (d) on the SE; and extending 25 m. in FON a NE direction from the line connecting these two points, and nearly 30 m. N and S along the meridian of Conseguina-point. See Conseguina. Chicarenepoint (e), on its W shore, 11 m. NE of Amapalapoint, is in N lat. 13° 17′ 5″, W long. 87° 42′ 39″. It may be described as an immense harbour, in which all the fleets in the world might ride in safety. Its entire length within land is not far from 100 m., by perhaps 50 m. in average breadth. It embraces several islands of considerable size, surrounded by water of such depth as to enable vessels to approach close in shore. The most important of these, from the circumstances of its size and the fact that it commands and is the key to the entire gulf, is the island of Tigre (T), belonging to Honduras. This island was the head-quarters of Drake during his operations in the South sea. It is about 30 m. in circumf., level near to the shore, but rising regularly and gradually to a perfect cone in the centre. Upon this island is situated the free port of Amapala. Its possession by any great maritime nation would enable it to exercise an influence and control over the commerce of the W part of the American continent, similar to that which the possession of Gibraltar by the English enables them to exercise over the commerce of Europe. Immediately to the N of Tigre island is Velasquez island (V), the largest island in the gulf. Between this latter island and Chicarene-point lie the islands of Disposicion (f), Violin (g), Sacate (h), and Perez (i). From the SE extremity of this gulf extends a large estuary or arm, called the Estero Real (m m m). Its course is precisely in the direction of the lake of Managua, which it approaches to within 15 or 20 m., and between which and the lake is the plain of Conejo, which is, in fact, a part of the plain of Leon, rising to an alt. not exceeding 200 ft. above the level of the sea, at any point between the lake of Managua and the gulf of F. This Estero has for most of its extent an ample depth of water: at 30 m. above its mouth it had 50 ft. There is a narrow bar at its mouth, upon which, at low tide, there is only 3 fath. The tide rises, however, about 10 ft.; and with artificial aid the bar could doubtless be passed at all times. The Estero preserves, for a long distance, a nearly uniform width of from 300 to 400 yds. Its banks are lined with mangroves, with a dense back-ground of mier trees. Captain Belcher, R. N., ascended the Estero for 30 m. in a vessel drawing 10 ft. of water. From the head of tide water in the Estero to the lake is not more than 20 or 25 m. Mr. Squier-whose description of the gulf and Estero we chiefly follow-argues that this is the best practicable route for a canal between the two great oceans. The advantages which would result to such a work from terminating in the gulf of F. are obviously very great; and it may be safely asserted that the passage from Lake-Managua to the gulf is eminently feasible. The length of the proposed line of communication for ships, from sea to sea, Mr. Squier estimates as follows. Length of the river San Juan, 90 miles. Part of the lake of Nicaragua to be traversed, Total, The Child From this must be deducted 25 m., in case the line is terminated at the port of Tamarinda. In case it should terminate in the gulf of Fonseca, it is possible it would not be many miles longer than to Realejo. The extent of actual canal would be to Realejo. an islet formed by the junction of the Dona Paula and Realejo rivers, and protected by a bluff mainland and the islands of Carden and Assassadens, from 120 to 140 m.; to Tamarinda, at the mouth of the small stream of that name, 90 to 110 m.; to Fonseca, 100 to 120 m. One marked advantage of the F. route is, that while the harbour of Realejo, though secure, is very small, the gulf of F., according to all accounts, would "float the navies of the world." Its entrance from the sea is guarded, but not wholly protected from the swell which enters from the ocean, by two volcanic islands, Conchaguita (a) 500 ft., and Manguera (b) 600 ft., between which rise three island-cones from the water, leaving deep channels between. Inside of these opens the spacious gulf, in the centre of which rises another volcanic cone, the island of Tigre, about which so much has been said of late. The situation of this island, as commanding the finest harbour of Central America, and the probable terminus of the Atlantic and Pacific canal, gives it importance.
FONSWAPRE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, arrond. of Saint-Nicholas. Pop. 89.

FONTA, a mountain of France, in the dep. of the Ariège, cant. of Oust, 14 m. S of Saint-Girons. It forms a spur of the Pyrenees, and runs a distance of about 8 m. in a N direction between the Salat and Bemajour.

FONTAINE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Verteillac and com. of Champagne. Pop. 1,001.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Chagny. Pop. in 1841, 1,639. Pozzolani mastic is manufactured here. -Also a canton and commune in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, arrond. of Belfort. The cant. comprises 29 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,499; in 1841, 9,431. The village is 7 m. ENE of Belfort. Pop. 342.—Also a village in the dep. of the Vendee, cant. and 4 m. S of Fontenay-le-Comte. Pop. 540. Common cloth is extensively manufactured here.
FONTAINE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, arrond. of Horion-Hozémont. Pop. 336. -Also a commune in the prov. of Namur, arrond. of Natoye. Pop. 163.
FONTAINE-BASSE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. of Fontaine-Valmont. Pop. 428.

FONTAINEBLEAU, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne. — The arrond. comprises an area of 134,673 hectares, and contains 7 cant., viz.: La Chapelle-la-Reine, Château-Landon, Fontainebleau, Lorrez-le-Bocage, Montereau, Moret, and Nemours. Pop. in 1831, 69,503; in 1846, 76,837.—The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,685; in 1841, 12,137.—The town, beautifully situated in the forest of the same name, 35 m. SSE of Paris, on the railway from Paris to Lyons, consists of a principal street with several smaller ones. Pop. 7,816. It has manufactories of porcelain. It is chiefly celebrated for its royal palace, which is built at the S end of the - FON town. In its external appearance this edifice is very irregular, having been erected at different times, and indeed consisting of four distinct chateaus, each of which has a garden. The whole, it is said, contains no fewer than about 900 apartments, most of which are fitted up in splendid style. The apartments in of France in 1814, and the bed-room in which he slept before he set off for Elba, are shown with interest to travellers. The chapel is extremely beautiful; and the theatre is remarkably elegant. Several pieces of water are seen from the palace, and some fine wooded hills, but there is nothing very remarkable about the grounds. The forest of F. contains about 25,000 acres. It covers several small hills and plains.
FONTAINE-LES-CROISILLES, a village of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Croisilles, 8 m. SE of Arras. Pop. 338. It has manufactories of linen and cambric, and of oil.
FONTAINE-DANIEL, a hamlet of France, in the dep., cant., and 3 m. SW of Mayenne. It has a large cotton-spinning mill and a cotton manufactory.

FONTAINE-LE-DUN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Yvetot. The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,804; in 1841, 10,942.—The town is 16 m. NNE of Yvetot. Pop. 482. It has a considerable trade in cattle, woollen, linen, and cotton fabrics, and hosiery. Fairs are held 4 times a-year.

FONTAINE-L'EVEQUE, a commune and town of France, capital of a canton in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 6 m. W of Charleroi, on the Bablone, an affluent of the Sambre. Pop. 2,862. It has extensive nail-works and several distilleries, and in the environs are several lime-quarries and kilns. Nails and iron form its chief articles of trade. This town was long a subject of contest between the counts of Hainault and princes of Liege. In 1757 it was taken by the Austrians, and in 1794 by the French.

FONTAINE-FRANCAISE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or, arrond. of Dijon. - The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,758; in 1841, 6,139.—The town is 25 m. NE of Dijon. Pop. 1,208. It has a fine castle, and a monument erected in honour of the victory gained in the vicinity by Henry IV. on the 5th June, 1595, over the duke of Mayenne and the Spanish forces. It has several blast-furnaces, and a manufactory of stone-ware. Fairs are held six times a-year.

FONTAINE-GUERARD, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Grainville, and com. of Radepont, 9 m. NW of Andelys. Pop. 143. It has cotton and wool spinning mills, and a manufactory of cloth.
FONTAINE-GUERIN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Beaufort, 6 m. S of Beauge. Pop. 1,102.
FONTAINE-HAUTE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. of Fontaine-Valmont. Pop. 50.
FONTAINE - LES - LUXEUIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Saône, cant. of Saint-Loup. 16 m. NW of Lure. Pop. 1,293. It has manufactories of pottery, and several lime and tile kilns.
FONTAINE - NOTRE - DAME, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. and 3 m. W of Cambrai. Pop. in 1841, 1,582.
FONTAINE-AU-PIRE, a commune of France. in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Carnières. Pop. 1,116.
FONTAINE-SAINT-GEORGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aube, cant. of Romillysur-Seine. Pop. 425.

FONTAINES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. of Neuville-sur-Saône, 7 m. N of Lyon. Pop. in 1841, 1,580.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Saint-Fargeau, 24 m. SSW of Ivigny. Pop. 1,056.—Also a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, arrond. of Clermont. Pop. 134.
FONTAINES (Trois), a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, arrond. of Trembleur. Pop. 233.
FONTAINES - SUR - SOMME, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Somme, cant. of Hellencourt. Pop. 1,301.

FONTANA, a town of the Pontifical states, in the legation and 20 m. SE of Bologna, and 10 m. SW of Imola, on the r. bank of the Santerno.—Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavoro, district and 9 m. SSW of Sora, near the confines of the Pontifical states. Pop. 2,500.
FONTANA-FREDDA, a village of Austrian- Lombardy, in the del. and 32 m. WSW of Udine, district and 5 m. WNW of Pordenone. It has an annual fair.
FONTANAMORA, a town of Piedmont, in Piedmont, in the prov. and 32 m. ESE of Aosta, on the r. bank of the Eiles. Pop. 1,400.
FONTANAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. and partido of Guadalajara, on an affluent of the Henares. Pop. 234.
FONTANAROSA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Principato-Ultra, district and 11 m. NW of San-Angelo-de-Lombardi, cant. and 5 m. W of Frigento. Pop. 4,000. It contains 2 parish churches.

FONTANELLA, a town of Austrian-Lombardy, in the prov. and 19 m. SSE of Bergamo, and 32 m. E of Milan. It was founded by the Bourguignons in 550, and was formerly a flourishing town; but the ravages of war and of pestilence have reduced its pop. to a number not exceeding 1,000.
FONTANELLATO, a village of Parma, in the district and 8 m. E of Borgo-san-Donino.
FONTANELLO, a town of the Sardinian states, in Piedmont, prov. and 15 m. SW of Vercelli, about 2 m, from the l. bank of the Po.
FONTANGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Salens, 5 m. SE of Sauriac, on the r. bank of the Maronne. Pop. 1,918. It has mines of coal and alum.

FONTANILS, a village of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. and 9 m. SE of Voiron, on the road from Lyon to Grenoble. The calcareous chain of Fontanils contains beds of fine marble, and large quantities of organic remains.
FONTARABIA. See FUENTERABIA.

FONT-COUVERT, a village of Sardinia, in Savoy, in the prov. of Maurienne, mand. and 3 m. WSW of St.-Jean-de-Maurienne. Pop. 1,307.- Also a village of France, in the dep. of the Charente- Inferieure, cant. and 3 m. NE of Saintes. Pop. 592. In the vicinity are the remains of an ancient aqueduct.
FONTE, a village of Austrian-Lombardy, in the prov. and 22 m. WNW of Treviso, near the r. bank of the Muson. Pop. 1,630.
FONTEARCADA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 16 m. N of Trancosa, and 31 m. SE of Lamego.
FONTE-BOA, a parish and town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, division of Rio Negro, between the rivers Jutahi and Juruha, and watered by several rivers which flow into the Amazon.

FONTECCHI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 16 m. SE of Aquila, near the l. bank of the Aterno. Pop. 1,000. It contains a collegiate church, and several convents, FON and has a manufactory of wax-lights. A fair is held here once a-vear.
FONTECHA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. SW of Alava, partido of Salinas-de-Sanana, on the l. bank of the Ebro. Pop. 246.
FONTELLAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Navarra, partido and 3 m. ESE of Tudela, near the r. bank of the Ebro. Pop. 140.
FONTELLO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 5 m. ENE of Lamego, on the l. bank of the Duero.

FONTENAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or, cant. and 3 m. NE of Moubart. Pop. 300. It has a paper-mill.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Loiret, cant. of Ferrieres, 13 m. NNE of Montargis, on the r. bank of the Loing. Pop. 536. It has a celebrated mineral spring. See also ROHAN-ROHAN and VOUTENAY.
FONTENAY-SOUS-BOIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. and 1 m. E of Vincennes. Pop. in 1831, 1,390; in 1841, 3,173.

FONTENAY-LE-COMTE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Vendée.—The arrond comprises an area of 216,288 hectares, and contains 9 cant., viz.: Chailléles-Marais, La Chataigneraie, Fontenay-le-Comte, L'Hermenault, Sainte-Hermine. Saint-Hilaire-des- Loges, Lucon, Maillerais, and Pouzanges. Pop. in 1831, 119,064; in 1841, 126,531.—The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,856; in 1841, 17,714. -The town is pleasantly situated on the slope of a valley, near the r. bank of the Vendee, 36 m. SE of Bourbon-Vendee, and 29 m. NE of La Rochelle. Pop. in 1789, 5,654; in 1821, 7,470; in 1831, 7,504; in 1841, 7,960. The streets are narrow, tortuous, and ill-paved, and the houses badly built; and, with the exception of the church of Notre-Dame, a fine edifice, the public halls, the college, and the ruins of a fortress, it contains no objects of architectural interest. It has manufactories of linen and common cloth, tanneries, and several breweries; and possesses a considerable entrepot trade in fire and cord-wood, staves, charcoal, and wine and grain from the southern deps. Fairs are held here 4 times a-year.—This town owes its existence to a fortress built by the counts of Poictiers. It was taken by the Huguenots in 1568, and again in 1570. In 1574 it fell into the hands of the Catholics under the duke of Montpensier, and was held by them until 1587, when it was taken by Henry IV. During the first French revolution it bore the name of Fontenay-le-Peuple, and was for some time the capital of the dep.

FONTENAY - AUX - ROSES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. and 13 m. NW of Sceaux, and 8 m. SW of Paris. Pop. 1,024. Roses and strawberries are extensively cultivated here. The village formerly possessed the privilege of furnishing a bouquet of roses to each member of parliament on the occasion of its annual opening.

FONTENAY-TRE'SIGNY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. and 6 m. W of Rozoy-en-Brie. Pop. 1,144. It contains a fine fountain, and ruins of a castle built by Philip the Bold.
FONTENELLE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur. Pop. 228.—Also a commune in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. of Nalinores. Pop. 66.
FONTENELLE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Antrain, 18 m. NW of Fougères. Pop. 1,104.
FONTENELLES, a village of France, in the dep. of the Vendee, cant. and 4 m. WSW of Bourbon- Vendee. Pop. 1,200.
FONTENILZ, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant, arrond. of Vieux-Genappe. ~
FONTENOILLE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, arrond. of Saint-Cecile. Pop. 558.

FONTENOY, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 4 m. SE of Tournai, near the l. bank of the Schelde. Pop. 750. It is noted for the defeat of the duke of Cumberland by the French under Marshal Saxe, which took place here on the 30th April 1745.

FONTENOY-LE-CHATEAU, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Bains, 21 m. SSW of Epinal, on the r. bank of the Toney. Pop. in 1841, 2,220. It has manufactories of kirschwasser. This town was formerly fortified. It belonged, at the beginning of the 17th cent., to the house of Croy.
FONTES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Montagnac. Pop. 1,004.—Also a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, comarca and 15 m. W of Villa-Real.

FONTEVRAULT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. and 9 m. SE of Saumur. Pop. in 1841, 3,639. It received its name from the old and wealthy Benedictine abbey founded by Robert d'Arbrissel in the 11th cent., and the abbesses of which were generally princesses. This institution was to a great extent destroyed at the first French revolution. The remaining portion now serves as a house-of-correction.
FONTIERS-CABARDES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aude, cant. of Saissac. Pop. 1,148.
FONTINELLA, a large village of Turkey in Europe, in Upper Wallachia, district of Teliorman, 66 m. SE of Slatina, on the l. bank of the Kalmatzia.
FONTIOYUELO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 36 m. NNW of Valladolid. Pop. 280.

FONTIVEROS, a small town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 28 m. NNW of Avila, and partido of Arevalo, on the r. bank of the Zapardiel. Pop. 1,250. It has 2 churches, 3 convents, and several fine fountains; and possesses lime and tilekilns. Fairs for sheep are held twice a-year.
FONTJOY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Moselle, cant. of Audun-le-Roman, 9 m. from Briey. Pop. 1,044. It has a gypsum-mill, and a manufactory of cloth.
FONTSTOWN, a parish of Queen's co. See Bal- LYADAMS.—Also a p. in co. Kildare, 4 m. NNE of Athy. Area 4,535 acres. Pop. in 1831, 753.

FONTVIEILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. and 6 m. NE of Arles, and 50 m. NW of Marseilles, on the brow of the hills in which the fine stone known by the name of the locality is quarried. Pop. in 1841, 2,142. It has several oil-mills.
FONZ, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 44 m. ESE of Huesca, and partido of Tamarite. Pop. 2,160. It has a parish-church and a customhouse, and possesses a considerable trade in cattle.
FONZALECHE, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 32 m. W of Logrono, and partido of Haro-y-sus-Barrios. Pop. 246.
FONZASO, a town of Austrian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 24 m. WSW of Belluno. Pop. 2,716.

FOO-CHOO-FU, a division of China, in the prov. of Keang-se. The division comprises 6 districts. The town which is its capital is situated in a fertile plain, on the l. bank of the Kan-kiang, 50 m. SE of Nan-chang, in N lat. 27° 56′ 24″, long. E of Peking 0° 10' 30". Previous to the incursions of the Manchoos, this town was one of the most flourishing in China.

FOO-CHUEN-CHOO, a district of China, in the prov. of Kwan-se, div. of Ping-lo-fu. The town which is its capital is 45 m. ENE of Ping-lo, on an FOR affluent of the Long-kiang, in N lat. 24° 33', E long. 111° 30′.
FOO-E-TING, a district of China, in the prov. of Kan-suh, div. of Kan-choo-fu. The town which is its capital is of the third class.
FOO-FUNG, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Shen-se, div. of Tung-seang-fu.
FOO-GAN-HEEN, a district of China, in the prov. of Fokeen, div. of Fuh-ning-fu. The town which is its capital is in N lat. 27° 4' 48", long. E of Peking 3° 18′ 40″.
FOO-KOW, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Honan, div. of Wei-hwuy-fu, on an affluent of the Ju-Chiu, 50 m. S of Kae-fung.
FOOK'S-MILLS, a village in the ps. of Ballylannan and Clongeen, co. Wexford. Area 11 acres. Pop. in 1841, 96.
FOO-KUH-HEEN, a district of China, in the prov. of Shen-se, div. of Yu-lin-fu. The town which is its capital is on the r. bank of the Hoang-ho, 100 m. NNE of Yu-lin.
FOOLADOO. See FULADU.
FOOLOW, a hamlet in the p. of Eyam, Derbyshire. Pop. 249.
FOOLSTONE, or FULSTONE, a township in the p. of Kirk-Burton, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 1,856.
FOO-MIN, a district and town of China, in the prov. and 18 m. NW of Yunnan, div. of Yunnan-fu, on the l. bank of the Po-lou-ho, in N lat. 25° 20', E long. 102° 48'.
FOO-NING, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Chih-le, div. of Yung-ping-fu, on an affluent of the gulf of Chih-le, in N lat. 39° 59', E long. 19° 18'.
FOO-PING, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Shen-se, div. of Se-gan-fu, on an affluent of the Hoei-ho, 18 m. SE of Yaou, and 40 m. N of Segan, in N lat. 34° 42′, E long. 109° 10′.
FOO-SHE-HEEN, a district of China, in the prov. of Shen-se, div. of Yen-gan-fu.
FOO-SHUN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Sze-chuen, div. of Seu-choo-fu.
FOOTA-JALLON. See FUTA-JALLON.
FOOTDEE, a considerable village of Aberdeenshire, at the mouth of the Dee, forming a suburb of New Aberdeen, and chiefly inhabited by fishermen.
FOOTHOG, a township in the p. of Cwmyoy, Hereford, 10 m. NNW of Abergavenny. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. in 1831, 127; in 1841, 132.

FOO-YANG, a district of China, in the prov. of Che-keang, div. of Hang-choo-fu. The town which is its capital is on the r. bank of the Tcheng-tangchiang, 24 m. SW of Hang-choo, in N lat. 30° 4' 57", E long. 119° 56' 37". It is surrounded by walls, and has adjacent a lofty tower of 7 stories. The neighbourhood is extremely fertile, producing corn, fruit, silk, and varieties of oleaginous plants in great abundance.
FOO-YANG-HEEN, a district of China, in the prov. of Honan, div. of Joo-ning-fu.

FORBACH, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of the Moselle, arrond. of Sarreguemines.—The cant. comprises 17 com. Pop. in 1841, 14,925.— The town, 7 m. NW of Sarreguemines, had a pop. of 3,082. It has manufactories of woollen cloths and tobacco-pipes.—Also a village of Baden, on the l. bank of the Murg, 7 m. SSE of Baden. Pop. 1,303.
FORBAN (TROU DU), a small port on the W coast of the island of Hayti, at the embouchure of the Montrony, 12 m. S of St. Marc.
FORBES, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 8 m. SE of Budweis.

FORBES (Mount), a lofty and prominent pyramidal summit crowning the most elevated SW extremity of Hardwick's range in SW. Australia. It is in about S lat. 30° 28', and nearly on the meridian of 150° E.
FORBES (Point), a cape on the E coast of Milby's island, in the gulf of Georgia.
FORBES'S ISLANDS, three islands in the S. Pacific ocean, in S lat. 12° 16′ 35″, E long. 143° 18′ 50", about 15 m. from Bolt-head, a cape on the NE coast of Australia.

FORCADOS (Rio-Dos), a river of Warri, in Upper Guinea, flowing into the bight of Benin, in N lat. 5° 45'. It is said to rise from a source far inland, and to have a very winding course. Its bar is 3 m. in length, and 1 m. broad, carrying 13 ft. at low-water. Within the bar it is about 2 m. broad, and navigable for vessels drawing 7 or 8 ft. of water. Its banks are covered with fine trees, and produce a species of coloured stones which the negroes use as beads. The coast both N and S of the Rio-F. presents a dense forest and tangled jungle rising from a narrow sandy beach.
FORCALL, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 44 m. N of Castellon-de-la-Plana, at the confluence of the Bergantes, Cantavieja, and Caldes. Pop. 2,000.

FORCALQUIER, an arrondissement, canton, and town of France, in the dep. of Basses-Alpes.-The arrond. comprises 6 cant., viz., Banon, Saint-Etienneles-Orgues, F., Manosque, Peyrius, and Reillane. Pop. in 1841, 36,118; in 1846, 36,231.—The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. of 9,256 in 1831; and of 9,461 in 1841.—The town, situated near the river Laye, had a pop. in 1841 of 1,998. Coal is mined in the vicinity.
FORCE (LA), a canton and small town of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, 6 m. W of Bergerac.
FORCELLA, a village of Naples, in Abruzzo-Ultra 1ma, 6 m. SE of Zeramo. Pop. 490.—Also a town in Abruzzo-Ultra 2da, 6 m. WNW of Aquila.
FORCETT, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. NNE of Richmond. Area 4,760 acres. Pop. in 1831, 430; in 1841, 451.
FORCHENBERG, a small town of Bavaria, 6 m. NE of Oettingen.
FORCHENSEE, a lake of Bavaria, 10 m. S of Traunstein.

FORCHHEIM, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Franconia, at the conflux of the Wiesent and Regnitz, 14 m. S of Bamberg. Pop. 3,000. It has some fortifications, but made no resistance to the French, who entered it in August 1796. It has glass-works, paper-mills, tanneries, and breweries.— Also a village of Saxony, in the circle of the Erzgebirge, 4 m. NNE of Lauterstein.—Also a village of Baden, 14 m. N by W of Freyburg.
FORCHTENAU, or Frakno-Allya, a village of Hungary, in the palatinate of Oedenburg, on the l. bank of the Wolka, 11 m. W of Oedenburg. Pop. 729. It is entirely surrounded by forests of chestnuts.
FORCHTENBERG, a town of Wurtemberg, circle of Jaxt, on the l. bank of the Kocher, 6 m. NNE of Oehringen. Pop. 1,164. There is a fine chateau of the princes of Hohenlohe-Oehringen in the vicinity.

FORD, a village 3 m. SW of Oulart, in co. Wexford. Pop. 327.—Also a village in the co. of Mid- Lothian, 10 m. SE of Edinburgh.—Also a parish of Northumberland, 7 m. NNW of Wooler. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,110; in 1841, 2,257.— Also a parish in Salop, 6 m. W by N of Shrewsbury. Area 2,140 acres. Pop. in 1831, 263; in 1841, 309. -Also a parish in Sussex, 3 m. SSW of Arundel. Area 480 acres. Pop. in 1831, 81; in 1841, 70.
FORD, a town of South Carolina, 6 m. NNW of Georgetown.
FORD (CAPE), a promontory on the NW coast of Australia, in S lat. 13° 24′ 35″, E long. 130° 52′ 20″. FOR
FORD'S ISLAND, a small island in the Atlantic, near the coast of S. Carolina, at the embouchure of the Santee.
FORDAN. See VORDON.
FORDE, a parish and village of Norway, in the bail. and 72 m. NNE of Bergen. Pop. 3,760.
FORDEN, a parish in the co. and 3 m. N of Montgomery. Pop. in 1831, 856; in 1841, 827.

FORDHAM, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 5 m. N of Newmarket. Area 4,050 acres. Pop. 1,416 .- Also a parish in Essex, 5 m. NW of Colchester. Area 2,460 acres. Pop. 739.—Also a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. S of Downham-market. Area 2,270 acres. Pop. 219.
FORDHAM, a village in Westchester co., in New York, U. S., on the line of the Haarlem railroad.

FORDINGBRIDGE, a town and parish in the co. and 19 m. NW of Southampton, situated on the river Avon, which is crossed by a bridge of 7 arches. Area of p. 5,720 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,822; in 1841, 3,073. The chief manufacture is tickings. Woollen and cotton yarn are also made in the neighbourhood. The town was considerably more extensive formerly, but has frequently suffered by fire.
FORDINGIANO, a small town of Sardinia, on the river Oristagni, 26 m. NE of Oristagni.
FORDINGTON, a parish of Dorsetshire, a t m. SE of Dorchester. Area 1,910 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,030; in 1841, 2,937.
FORDON, a small town of Prussian Poland, on the Vistula, 7 m. E by N of Bromberg. Pop. 2,080, of whom nearly one-third are Jews.
